formulation: vTaiwan was built inside a polity whose legitimacy was not itself the question at stake. The populist challenge described in these sessions targets the legitimacy of the host institution — citizens are not asking the EU for better policy, they are asking whether the EU deserves to make policy at all. Can a deliberative platform channel grievance that is aimed at the platform's own host institution, or does your model require a prior ground of shared institutional trust that is precisely what populism has dissolved?
context_narrative: Across two panels, the conference wrestled with populism as both democratic signal and institutional threat. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez traced its roots to material betrayal — governing parties abandoning working-class demands — while Petr Pavel insisted populism is not inherently dangerous and becomes a problem only when turned against democratic institutions themselves. Manfred Weber defended the European center's firewall strategy, arguing concrete results on migration and trade could reclaim populist voters. But in a separate session, Petr Macinka challenged the EU's democratic credentials directly — the European Parliament cannot even initiate legislation — leaving unresolved whether the institutions meant to answer populism can demonstrate the democratic legitimacy that populism demands.

selected_quotes.quote: Populism should not automatically be labeled as something bad; listening to people's frustrations and translating them into policy is the very meaning of politics — the problem begins only when populism is turned against the institutions that protect democracy.

The sovereign nation-state is the only platform that can guarantee democracy because it is the only way to maintain cultural continuity and keep power within limited, accountable institutions; democracy cannot fully exist at the supranational level.

The European Parliament is the only parliament in the world that cannot initiate legislation, which fundamentally undermines the claim that the EU is democratically governed in the same way as a nation-state.

The EPP maintains a clear firewall against cooperation with parties that are not pro-Europe, pro-Ukraine, and pro-rule of law, and he personally expelled Viktor Orbán from the EPP for violating these principles.
selected_quotes.flavor: rejecting the previous claim, speaking with intensity

selected_quotes.quote: Is there a meaningful distinction between responsive populism — listening to and translating citizens' frustrations into policy — and dangerous populism — turning grievances against democratic institutions — and if so, where is the line?

rationale: The formulation activates Tang on deliberative mechanisms and participation design while pushing hard into her stretch territory — whether the Taiwan model exports — by confronting her with a context where the institution itself, not its policy outputs, is what citizens reject, forcing her to address whether process-based legitimacy can bootstrap itself in the absence of the shared institutional trust her platforms have always presupposed.
